preFix.title: Triple glazing vs secondary glazing: the cost-benefit comparison
preFix.subtitle: When new windows are justified and when secondary glazing is the better financial decision
preFix.excerpt: Replacing double-glazed units with triple glazing costs 8-15 times more than adding secondary glazing to the same window. The thermal improvement from triple glazing over double is modest: U-value drops from 1.4 to 0.7 W/m²·K, saving perhaps 60-90 kWh per window per year. This explains where each option makes sense.
preFix.sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Approved Document L1B (2022) for replacement window U-value targets in existing dwellings. Energy Saving Trust glazing cost and savings estimates. Glass and Glazing Federation guidance on window U-value certification. Historic England glazing guidance for listed buildings.

content.text: The UK window market presents two distinct pathways for reducing heat loss from poor glazing. The first is replacing the window entirely with a triple-glazed unit. The second is adding internal secondary glazing to the existing window. The thermal outcomes are similar for a single window. The cost difference is large.

Typical comparison for a 1.2 m x 1.5 m window (1.8 m² glazed area): replacing existing double glazing (U = 1.4 W/m²·K) with triple (U = 0.7 W/m²·K) costs 600-1,200 pounds installed. Adding secondary glazing to the same window costs 80-180 pounds for a DIY magnetic or clip-in system. Both reduce heat loss through the window by roughly 200-300 kWh per year at an average temperature difference of 10°C.
When triple glazing makes sense

The existing frames need replacement regardless: rotting frames, failing seals, or misting between panes all justify replacement on condition grounds alone. At that point, specifying triple glazing instead of double adds roughly 20-30% to the window cost.
The property is a new build or major extension where a certified window centre-pane
U-value
of 0.8 or below is needed to meet the design SAP calculation.

Acoustic improvement as well as thermal: triple glazing cuts sound better than double, especially in the 100-1000 Hz range. For a property on a busy road or near an airport, the noise reduction alone can justify the cost.
North-facing large windows in an otherwise highly insulated new build where the glazing is the dominant heat-loss surface.
When secondary glazing makes sense
Listed buildings and conservation areas: you normally need planning permission to replace original windows. Secondary glazing is usually allowed without consent. It is reversible and sits inside.

Existing frames are in good condition but the sealed units are cold: the frame is structurally sound but single-glazed or old double-glazed. Adding secondary glazing doubles the thermal resistance of the glazing without touching the frame.
Budget is constrained: secondary glazing on six windows costs less than replacing one window with triple glazing. For the same spend, secondary glazing covers more windows across the house.
Rental property: secondary glazing is reversible and does not alter the window frame. This is useful when a lease restricts changes to the property.
Secondary glazing types

Magnetic frame system: a rigid acrylic panel held in an aluminium frame that clips magnetically to a steel strip bonded to the window reveal. Removable in seconds for summer. Cost per window: 80-160 pounds DIY-installed.

Hinged aluminium frame with safety glass: a full secondary system opening inward on hinges, with slim-section aluminium framing. Allows full opening of the primary window for ventilation. Cost per window: 250-600 pounds professionally installed.

Polythene film on a compression frame: sold as a temporary draught-proofing kit. Reduces U-value and draught but the film scratches and yellows within two to three years. For permanent use, the acrylic panel systems perform better.
Worked example: 1990s semi, eight double-glazed windows

Existing: eight double-glazed units, total glazed area 12 m², U-value 1.4 W/m²·K. Annual heat loss through glazing: approximately 2,400 kWh at 10°C average temperature difference over the heating season.

Option A: replace all eight windows with triple glazing. Cost: approximately 7,000-10,000 pounds. New U-value: 0.7 W/m²·K. Annual saving: 1,200 kWh, roughly 70 pounds at 6p/kWh gas. Payback: 100-140 years. Justified only if the frames need replacing regardless.

Option B: secondary glazing on all eight windows. Cost: approximately 900-1,400 pounds DIY-installed magnetic systems. Combined U-value of existing double plus secondary panel: approximately 0.7 W/m²·K (nearly equivalent to triple glazing). Annual saving: similar 1,200 kWh. Payback: 13-20 years. A better financial case.

attrs.fix: Apply self-adhesive foam strip to the secondary frame perimeter before installing. The gap between the two panes must be sealed.
attrs.cause: Air is leaking into the gap between the two glazing layers; the secondary system is not airtight at its frame perimeter
attrs.symptom: Secondary glazing panel mists up on the inside (between primary and secondary)

attrs.fix: Check the window specification: warm-edge spacer bars (silicone or foam-based) significantly reduce frame-edge heat loss compared to aluminium spacers. Specify warm-edge on any future order.
attrs.cause: Edge-seal thermal bridging: the glass spacer bar between panes is conducting heat at the frame perimeter
attrs.symptom: New triple-glazed windows feel cold at the frame edge despite having low centre-pane U-values
